title: Apple Neural Engine, CoreML & Profiling on Apple Silicon
description: The Apple Neural Engine (ANE) is invisible to PyTorch and MLX — to reach it you go through CoreML. Learn the conversion path, heterogeneous CPU/GPU/ANE execution, and how to profile with Instruments and torch.mps.profiler.

sections.title: What the Apple Neural Engine Is (and Isn't)
sections.content:
### A third compute unit you've been ignoring

Every M-series chip ships with three programmable compute engines: the **CPU**, the **GPU**, and the **Apple Neural Engine (ANE)**. The first two you already use. The ANE is a fixed-function matrix accelerator with its own SRAM and its own scheduling — and from PyTorch or MLX, **you cannot directly target it**. The only public software path to the ANE is through **CoreML**.

A quick architectural sketch:

```
 M-series SoC
  +-----------------+   +------------------+   +------------------+
  |   CPU cores     |   |   GPU cores      |   |   ANE (16-core)  |
  |  (P + E)        |   |  Metal           |   |  fp16 / int8     |
  |  any precision  |   |  fp32/fp16/bf16  |   |  matmul + conv   |
  +-----------------+   +------------------+   +------------------+
          \                       |                      /
           +----------- Unified Memory (LPDDR) ---------+
```

### What the ANE is good at

- **fp16 inference** of typical convolutional and transformer architectures, especially at small-to-medium batch sizes.
- **int8 quantized inference** with very high efficiency (the ANE's int8 path is its sweet spot).
- **Sustained, low-power inference.** The ANE is dramatically more efficient than the GPU per token. A 7B model running on the GPU will saturate the chip's thermals and drain a laptop battery; the same model running on the ANE may pull a fraction of the power.
- **Latency-sensitive workloads.** First-token latency on the ANE is often lower than on the GPU because the ANE doesn't need to warm up the GPU clocks.

### What the ANE can't do

- **Training.** The ANE is inference-only. There's no public API to backprop through it.
- **fp32.** The ANE accepts fp16 and int8/int4 inputs; CoreML will downcast a fp32 model to fp16 for ANE dispatch (potentially losing accuracy).
- **Custom ops or shaders.** No analogue of `torch.mps.compile_shader`. You get the ops CoreML supports — that's it.
- **Models that don't fit its op coverage.** Anything with weird control flow, dynamic shapes, or custom kernels falls back to GPU or CPU.
- **Top peak FLOPS.** The GPU has higher peak throughput. For very large batches or huge transformers, the GPU wins on raw speed; the ANE wins on efficiency.

### When the ANE is the right answer

The practical rubric:

- **On-device inference of small-to-medium vision/audio models** (image classifiers, speech-to-text up to a few hundred million parameters): almost always use the ANE.
- **On-device inference of LLMs up to ~3B parameters**, especially with int8 quantization: the ANE is competitive and saves battery.
- **Anything bigger than ~7B parameters or fp16-only**: the GPU (via MLX or PyTorch+MPS) is usually the better choice today.
- **Training, custom ops, research code**: never the ANE — use GPU.

> **Quick check.** Why can't PyTorch dispatch directly to the ANE? *(Apple has not exposed a low-level ANE API; the only public path is the CoreML runtime, which makes its own dispatch decisions.)*

sections.title: PyTorch -> CoreML: The Conversion Path
sections.content:
### `coremltools` is the bridge

The canonical path from a trained PyTorch model to an ANE-eligible CoreML model is:

```
 PyTorch nn.Module
    |
    |  torch.jit.trace(model, example_inputs)   OR  torch.export.export(...)
    v
  TorchScript / ExportedProgram
    |
    |  coremltools.convert(...)
    v
  .mlpackage (CoreML model, Core ML 7+)
```

A minimal end-to-end conversion:

```python
import torch
import coremltools as ct

class TinyClassifier(torch.nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.net = torch.nn.Sequential(
            torch.nn.Conv2d(3, 16, 3, padding=1),
            torch.nn.ReLU(),
            torch.nn.AdaptiveAvgPool2d(1),
            torch.nn.Flatten(),
            torch.nn.Linear(16, 10),
        )
    def forward(self, x): return self.net(x)

model = TinyClassifier().eval()
example = torch.randn(1, 3, 224, 224)
traced  = torch.jit.trace(model, example)

mlmodel = ct.convert(
    traced,
    inputs=[ct.TensorType(name="image", shape=example.shape)],
    convert_to="mlprogram",            # modern format; required for ANE on newer OS
    compute_units=ct.ComputeUnit.ALL,    # let CoreML pick CPU/GPU/ANE per op
    minimum_deployment_target=ct.target.macOS14,
)
mlmodel.save("tiny_classifier.mlpackage")
```

### Knobs that actually matter

- **`convert_to="mlprogram"`** is the modern format and is required if you want ANE engagement on macOS 13+ / iOS 16+. The older `"neuralnetwork"` format is legacy.
- **`compute_units`** is a hint about which engines CoreML may use. Options:
  - `ALL` — CoreML picks per op. Default. Right answer 95% of the time.
  - `CPU_AND_NE` — exclude the GPU. Useful for sustained inference on battery.
  - `CPU_AND_GPU` — exclude the ANE. Useful for benchmarking or for models the ANE handles poorly.
  - `CPU_ONLY` — for debugging numerics.
- **`minimum_deployment_target`** — set to the oldest OS you care about. Newer targets unlock more ops on the ANE.
- **`inputs=` shape** can include `ct.RangeDim` for dynamic shapes, but flexible shapes often disable ANE dispatch. If you can fix the shape, do.

### Loading and running the model

```python
import coremltools as ct
import numpy as np

m = ct.models.MLModel("tiny_classifier.mlpackage")
img = np.random.randn(1, 3, 224, 224).astype(np.float32)
out = m.predict({"image": img})
print(out)
```

### Verifying which compute unit was used

CoreML's per-op dispatch decisions are not exposed by `predict()`. You see them either in **Xcode** (open the `.mlpackage`, click **Performance**) or by running a profiling trace (next section). A quick sanity check is to run the model with three different `compute_units` settings and compare:

```python
for cu in [ct.ComputeUnit.CPU_ONLY,
           ct.ComputeUnit.CPU_AND_GPU,
           ct.ComputeUnit.CPU_AND_NE,
           ct.ComputeUnit.ALL]:
    m = ct.models.MLModel("tiny_classifier.mlpackage", compute_units=cu)
    # warm + time many predictions ...
```

If `CPU_AND_NE` is dramatically faster than `CPU_ONLY`, the ANE is doing real work. If it's the same, your model isn't reaching the ANE — usually a sign of an unsupported op or a flexible-shape input.

### Op-coverage gotchas

The ANE is picky. Common reasons CoreML won't schedule onto it:

- **Dynamic shapes** (a leading batch dim with `RangeDim` is OK; arbitrary spatial dims usually aren't).
- **fp32-only ops.** The ANE wants fp16. CoreML downcasts most weights, but custom ops that demand fp32 force CPU/GPU fallback.
- **Unusual layouts.** Channels-first vs. channels-last conversions can prevent ANE fusion.
- **Long sequences** (some attention patterns at very long context).

The "Performance" tab in Xcode highlights every op that didn't reach the ANE and tells you why. It's the most efficient way to debug coverage.

> **Gotcha.** A model can `compute_units=ALL` and still run **entirely on the CPU** if the ops aren't ANE-friendly. Speed alone is a poor proxy for engagement; verify in Xcode Performance.

sections.title: Mac vs iOS Deployment: One Model, Different Knobs
sections.content:
### The same `.mlpackage` runs everywhere

A CoreML `.mlpackage` produced on a Mac runs unchanged on iPhone, iPad, Vision Pro, and Apple TV. The runtime adapts to whichever ANE/GPU/CPU is present. This is one of CoreML's killer features compared to wiring up TFLite + GPU delegates by hand.

That said, *runtime knobs* differ between Mac and iOS:

| Concern | macOS | iOS / iPadOS |
|---|---|---|
| Default `compute_units` | `ALL` | `ALL` |
| Battery-life tradeoff | usually negligible | dominant — prefer `CPU_AND_NE` |
| Model-loading latency | fast (cold cache ~50ms) | slower (~hundreds of ms first time) |
| Memory pressure | shared with apps | app jetsam limits much tighter |
| Background inference | allowed | very restricted |
| `mlpackage` precompilation | optional | recommended; happens at app install |

### Fixed-shape vs. flexible-shape

On iOS, fixed-shape models load faster, hit the ANE more reliably, and give predictable latency. On Mac you can be looser, but the same advice applies. If you have batch=1 and a known image size, declare it:

```python
ct.TensorType(name="image", shape=(1, 3, 224, 224))   # fixed, ANE-friendly
```

Not:

```python
ct.TensorType(name="image", shape=(
    ct.RangeDim(1, 8),       # variable batch
    3,
    ct.RangeDim(128, 512),   # variable spatial -> often kicks off ANE
    ct.RangeDim(128, 512),
))
```

### Compilation artifacts

A `.mlpackage` is the *source* form (a directory containing the model + metadata). At runtime CoreML compiles it into an `.mlmodelc` directory of GPU shaders and ANE descriptors. On Mac this happens transparently; on iOS the system caches it after the first compile. For latency-sensitive apps you can ship a precompiled `.mlmodelc` directly.

### Multi-engine model packs

For production iOS/Mac apps that need both "fast on launch" and "highest quality after warm-up", a common pattern is to ship two models:

- A **small** model with `compute_units=ALL` for snappy first responses.
- A **larger** model that stays on the **GPU** (or ANE) for higher quality once warmed up.

CoreML doesn't manage this for you — you orchestrate it in app code — but it's a pattern worth knowing.

> **Quick check.** Why does declaring fixed input shapes help ANE engagement? *(Many ANE op kernels are shape-specialized at compile time. Flexible-shape ops force CoreML to pick more general fallbacks, often on GPU or CPU.)*

sections.title: Profiling on Apple Silicon
sections.content:
### Four tools, four perspectives

No single profiler shows you everything. The combo I reach for:

1. **Xcode Instruments** — system-wide, GPU-deep, ANE-aware. The flagship tool.
2. **`powermetrics`** — terminal, sampling-based, energy and per-engine utilisation.
3. **PyTorch's MPS profiler** — operator-level inside Python.
4. **MLX's metal trace tools** — for MLX programs.

### Xcode Instruments

```
Xcode -> Open Developer Tool -> Instruments -> [Metal System Trace]
```

The templates worth knowing:

- **Metal System Trace** — the universal GPU/CPU timeline. Shows command-buffer encoding, GPU kernel execution, CPU stalls. Works for any process using Metal — including PyTorch and MLX.
- **Core ML** — when profiling a CoreML model: shows per-op compute-unit assignment, ANE/GPU/CPU engagement, layer-level timing. Best way to answer "is this actually running on the ANE?".
- **Time Profiler** — CPU sampling. For when the bottleneck is Python or NumPy, not GPU.
- **Energy** — battery-life impact. Useful when the choice between GPU and ANE is power-driven.

To profile a Python script, attach Instruments to the python interpreter while the script runs (or launch it from Instruments). For PyTorch+MPS the Metal System Trace gives you per-kernel timings.

### `powermetrics`

```bash
sudo powermetrics --samplers gpu_power,ane_power,cpu_power -i 200
```

This dumps per-200ms samples of GPU/ANE/CPU active residency, frequency, and energy. Two things it tells you that nothing else does:

- **Whether the ANE is actually running.** "ANE Power" jumping from 0 mW to a few hundred mW is the cleanest signal that ANE dispatch is happening.
- **Sustained vs. burst.** A 10-second run that holds 8W on the GPU might be hitting thermal throttling; `powermetrics` shows the frequency drop.

Lightweight, no instrumentation, runs alongside any workload.

### PyTorch MPS profiler

PyTorch ships a profiler that integrates the MPS backend:

```python
import torch
from torch.profiler import profile, ProfilerActivity

model = ...   # already on 'mps'

with profile(activities=[ProfilerActivity.CPU, ProfilerActivity.MPS],
             record_shapes=True) as prof:
    for _ in range(20):
        out = model(x)
        torch.mps.synchronize()

print(prof.key_averages().table(sort_by="self_mps_time_total", row_limit=15))
prof.export_chrome_trace("trace.json")     # open in chrome://tracing
```

This is the right tool for "which PyTorch op is slow on MPS?". It does *not* show ANE activity (PyTorch doesn't talk to the ANE).

### MLX profiling

MLX exposes `mx.metal.start_capture` / `stop_capture` to write a `.gputrace` file viewable in Xcode. The pattern:

```python
import mlx.core as mx

mx.metal.start_capture("trace.gputrace")
for _ in range(10):
    out = model(x)
    mx.eval(out)
mx.metal.stop_capture()
```

Open `trace.gputrace` in Xcode and you get a full Metal frame capture of the MLX session — the same view as a custom MSL kernel.

### Putting it together: "where does my model actually run?"

The canonical workflow when you suspect a model isn't using the engine you expected:

1. **Run with `compute_units=ALL`** and time it.
2. **Run with `compute_units=CPU_ONLY`** to set the floor.
3. **Run with `compute_units=CPU_AND_NE`** and `CPU_AND_GPU`** separately. The fastest of the two tells you which engine is doing the heavy lifting.
4. **Open the model in Xcode -> Performance**. Click each layer; the right pane shows compute-unit assignment.
5. **`powermetrics`** while running: confirm ANE Power > 0 if you expected ANE engagement.

If step 3 shows `CPU_AND_NE` matching `CPU_ONLY`, the ANE is *not* engaging — go to step 4 to find which op is blocking it.

> **Gotcha.** PyTorch's MPS profiler measures *queue submission* by default; for accurate kernel timings you need `record_shapes=True` and the synchronize calls shown above. Without sync, the timer captures Python/CPU dispatch time instead of GPU compute time.

sections.title: Quantization on Apple Silicon
sections.content:
### Two roads, same destination

Quantization on Apple Silicon happens through two parallel ecosystems:

- **CoreML** — `coremltools.optimize` provides post-training and palettized quantization at fp16/int8/int4. Best when you're going to run the model through the ANE.
- **MLX** — `mx.quantize` and the `mlx-lm` quantizer; weight-only block quantization at 4 / 8 bits. Best when you'll stay in MLX/Python.

They're not interoperable: an MLX 4-bit checkpoint is not a CoreML model. Pick one based on where the model will run.

### CoreML quantization

The modern API uses the `coremltools.optimize.coreml` module:

```python
import coremltools as ct
import coremltools.optimize.coreml as cto

mlmodel = ct.models.MLModel("tiny_classifier.mlpackage")

# Linear weight quantization to int8
config = cto.OptimizationConfig(
    global_config=cto.OpLinearQuantizerConfig(
        mode="linear_symmetric", weight_threshold=512
    )
)
q_model = cto.linear_quantize_weights(mlmodel, config=config)
q_model.save("tiny_classifier_int8.mlpackage")
```

For more aggressive compression, **palettization** clusters weights into 16/32/64 unique values per layer (effectively 4–6 bit weights with a lookup table) — extremely ANE-friendly.

### MLX quantization

```bash
mlx_lm.convert \
    --hf-path mistralai/Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3 \
    --mlx-path ./Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3-q4 \
    -q --q-bits 4 --q-group-size 64
```

Programmatic API:

```python
import mlx.core as mx
import mlx.nn as nn

model = ...  # MLX model with fp16/fp32 weights
nn.quantize(model, group_size=64, bits=4)
```

The quantization is GPTQ-style block quantization: every group of 64 elements gets a shared fp16 scale and zero point. q-group-size trades quality (smaller = better) for storage (smaller = larger files because of more scales).

### What "int8" and "int4" actually buy you

For inference, the two interesting numbers are **memory bandwidth** and **memory footprint**:

| Precision | Bytes per weight | Bandwidth read time (vs. fp32) | Typical quality drop |
|---|---|---|---|
| fp32 | 4 | 1.0x | baseline |
| fp16 | 2 | 0.5x | negligible for inference |
| int8 | 1 | 0.25x | small (~0.5–1.0 perplexity points) |
| int4 | 0.5 | 0.125x | mild (~1–3 perplexity points) for grouped quant |

Decoder-style LLM inference at batch 1 is overwhelmingly bandwidth-bound, so quantization speedups roughly track the bandwidth column.

### Choosing

- **Vision/audio model going to ANE**: int8 via CoreML. Rarely a noticeable quality drop.
- **Local LLM inference**: 4-bit via mlx-lm. Best speed/quality on Apple Silicon today.
- **Production iOS app where a few hundred KB of binary matters**: palettize via CoreML.
- **Training**: stick with fp16/bf16. Quantization-aware training is possible but rarely worth the complexity on Mac.

> **Gotcha.** Don't mix quantization methods. You cannot quantize the same model in CoreML *and* in MLX and expect equivalent results — the math, the group sizes, and even the calibration data flow differ. Pick one path, evaluate quality, ship it.
sections.code:
# Convert a small PyTorch CNN to CoreML, run with 'ALL' compute units,
# and time it against a CPU-only baseline. Also touches the MPS profiler.
import time
import numpy as np
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

import coremltools as ct

# 1. A tiny PyTorch model
class TinyCNN(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.net = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Conv2d(3, 32, 3, padding=1), nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Conv2d(32, 64, 3, padding=1), nn.ReLU(),
            nn.AdaptiveAvgPool2d(1),
            nn.Flatten(),
            nn.Linear(64, 10),
        )
    def forward(self, x):
        return self.net(x)

model = TinyCNN().eval()
example = torch.randn(1, 3, 224, 224)
traced  = torch.jit.trace(model, example)

# 2. Convert to CoreML mlprogram
mlmodel = ct.convert(
    traced,
    inputs=[ct.TensorType(name="image", shape=example.shape)],
    convert_to="mlprogram",
    compute_units=ct.ComputeUnit.ALL,
    minimum_deployment_target=ct.target.macOS14,
)
mlmodel.save("tiny_cnn.mlpackage")

# 3. Benchmark all four compute_units settings
img = example.numpy().astype(np.float32)

def bench(cu, n=50):
    m = ct.models.MLModel("tiny_cnn.mlpackage", compute_units=cu)
    for _ in range(5):  # warm
        m.predict({"image": img})
    t0 = time.perf_counter()
    for _ in range(n):
        m.predict({"image": img})
    return (time.perf_counter() - t0) / n * 1000  # ms

for cu in [
    ct.ComputeUnit.CPU_ONLY,
    ct.ComputeUnit.CPU_AND_GPU,
    ct.ComputeUnit.CPU_AND_NE,
    ct.ComputeUnit.ALL,
]:
    print(f"{cu.name:<14}: {bench(cu):.3f} ms / inference")

# 4. PyTorch MPS profiler on the original model (for comparison)
if torch.backends.mps.is_available():
    from torch.profiler import profile, ProfilerActivity
    m_mps = TinyCNN().to('mps').eval()
    x = example.to('mps')
    with profile(activities=[ProfilerActivity.CPU, ProfilerActivity.MPS]) as prof:
        for _ in range(20):
            _ = m_mps(x)
            torch.mps.synchronize()
    print("\nTop ops by MPS time:")
    print(prof.key_averages().table(sort_by="self_mps_time_total", row_limit=10))

references.title: Apple Neural Engine (Apple WWDC sessions index)

quiz.question: Why can't PyTorch dispatch operations directly to the Apple Neural Engine?
options: PyTorch dropped ANE support in version 2.0
Apple has not exposed a public low-level ANE API; the only public path is the CoreML runtime
The ANE is GPU-only and PyTorch is CPU-only
PyTorch tensors aren't compatible with the ANE's instruction set
quiz.explanation: Apple has never published a low-level API for the ANE. The only documented path is via CoreML, which converts a model description into ANE descriptors at load time. Both PyTorch+MPS and MLX target the GPU; neither talks to the ANE.

quiz.question: Which `compute_units` setting tells CoreML it may use any combination of CPU, GPU, and ANE?
quiz.explanation: `ComputeUnit.ALL` lets CoreML schedule ops across all three engines per its own heuristics. `CPU_AND_GPU` excludes the ANE; `CPU_AND_NE` excludes the GPU; `AUTO` is not a real option.

quiz.question: Which file format should you use today (`convert_to=...`) for ANE-eligible CoreML models?
quiz.explanation: `convert_to="mlprogram"` is the modern format and is required for ANE engagement on macOS 13+ / iOS 16+. The older `"neuralnetwork"` format still works but won't reach newer ANE features.

quiz.question: You set `compute_units=ALL` and the inference is fast — but you want to verify the ANE is actually doing the work. Which is the cleanest single check?
options: Run powermetrics with `--samplers ane_power` and watch ANE Power jump above zero
Look at GPU utilization in Activity Monitor
quiz.explanation: `powermetrics --samplers ane_power` reads the SoC's per-engine power counters. ANE Power above ~0 mW during inference is direct evidence the engine is active. Activity Monitor doesn't show ANE; printing on the model object doesn't either.

quiz.question: Why do flexible input shapes (e.g. `RangeDim` for spatial dimensions) often disable ANE dispatch?
options: Many ANE kernels are shape-specialized at compile time; flexible shapes force more general fallbacks (often GPU or CPU)
CoreML refuses to load flexible-shape models
quiz.explanation: ANE op kernels are pre-compiled for specific shapes. Flexible (`RangeDim`) shapes force CoreML to pick general-purpose kernels that often live on the GPU/CPU. Fixed shapes are far more likely to land on the ANE.

quiz.question: On an LLM at batch=1, why does 4-bit quantization typically make inference faster, not slower, despite the dequantize step?
options: 4-bit ops use the ANE; fp16 doesn't
Decoder inference at batch=1 is bandwidth-bound; reading 4-bit weights is 4x cheaper, and the dequantize-to-fp16 happens in registers
PyTorch skips half the layers under quantization
It only seems faster because the model is smaller
quiz.explanation: At batch=1 the matmul is bandwidth-bound: most time is spent fetching weights. Going from fp16 to int4 cuts weight memory traffic 4x. The dequantize is fused into the matmul kernel and adds negligible compute compared to the bandwidth saved.

quiz.question: Which profiling tool is best for figuring out 'which PyTorch op is slow on MPS?'
options: torch.profiler.profile with ProfilerActivity.MPS and torch.mps.synchronize calls
quiz.explanation: PyTorch's profiler with the MPS activity exposes per-op MPS time. The synchronize calls are essential — without them you measure dispatch latency, not GPU compute. Xcode's Core ML template is for CoreML models; powermetrics is system-level energy.

exercise.title: Convert a PyTorch model to CoreML and benchmark per compute unit
exercise.description:
Take a small PyTorch CNN, convert it to CoreML's `mlprogram` format, and benchmark the same `.mlpackage` under four `compute_units` settings (CPU_ONLY, CPU_AND_GPU, CPU_AND_NE, ALL). Print the per-inference time for each setting and identify which engine is doing the heavy lifting.

**Requirements:**
- Build a tiny CNN (a few Conv2d + ReLU + AdaptiveAvgPool + Linear) in PyTorch and put it in `eval()` mode.
- Trace it with `torch.jit.trace` on a fixed-shape input (1, 3, 224, 224).
- Convert with `ct.convert(..., convert_to="mlprogram", minimum_deployment_target=ct.target.macOS14)`.
- Save to `tiny_cnn.mlpackage`.
- Reload the model with each of the four `compute_units` values, warm up with 5 predictions, then time 50 predictions and print mean ms/inference.
- Report which `compute_units` setting was fastest and reason about whether the ANE is engaged (i.e. is `CPU_AND_NE` faster than `CPU_ONLY`?).

**Hints:**
- Install: `pip install coremltools torch torchvision`.
- The first `.predict()` after a load is slow (compile-on-load); always warm up before timing.
- Use `time.perf_counter()` for timing.
- If you have `powermetrics`, run `sudo powermetrics --samplers ane_power -i 200` in another terminal during the `CPU_AND_NE` run to confirm ANE engagement directly.

# 4. Benchmark function

    """Reload the saved package with the given compute_units and time n predictions."""
    raise NotImplementedError

# 5. Run on all four settings
# for cu in [ct.ComputeUnit.CPU_ONLY, ct.ComputeUnit.CPU_AND_GPU,
#            ct.ComputeUnit.CPU_AND_NE, ct.ComputeUnit.ALL]:
#     ms = bench(cu)
#     print(f"{cu.name:<14}: {ms:.3f} ms / inference")
exercise.solution:
import time
import numpy as np
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import coremltools as ct

class TinyCNN(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.net = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Conv2d(3, 32, 3, padding=1), nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Conv2d(32, 64, 3, padding=1), nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Conv2d(64, 64, 3, padding=1), nn.ReLU(),
            nn.AdaptiveAvgPool2d(1),
            nn.Flatten(),
            nn.Linear(64, 10),
        )
    def forward(self, x):
        return self.net(x)

model = TinyCNN().eval()
example = torch.randn(1, 3, 224, 224)
traced  = torch.jit.trace(model, example)

mlmodel = ct.convert(
    traced,
    inputs=[ct.TensorType(name="image", shape=example.shape)],
    convert_to="mlprogram",
    compute_units=ct.ComputeUnit.ALL,
    minimum_deployment_target=ct.target.macOS14,
)
mlmodel.save("tiny_cnn.mlpackage")

img = example.numpy().astype(np.float32)

def bench(cu, n=50, warm=5):
    m = ct.models.MLModel("tiny_cnn.mlpackage", compute_units=cu)
    for _ in range(warm):
        m.predict({"image": img})
    t0 = time.perf_counter()
    for _ in range(n):
        m.predict({"image": img})
    return (time.perf_counter() - t0) / n * 1000  # ms

results = {}
for cu in [
    ct.ComputeUnit.CPU_ONLY,
    ct.ComputeUnit.CPU_AND_GPU,
    ct.ComputeUnit.CPU_AND_NE,
    ct.ComputeUnit.ALL,
]:
    ms = bench(cu)
    results[cu.name] = ms
    print(f"{cu.name:<14}: {ms:7.3f} ms / inference")

fastest = min(results, key=results.get)
print(f"\nFastest: {fastest} ({results[fastest]:.3f} ms)")

speedup_ne_vs_cpu = results['CPU_ONLY'] / results['CPU_AND_NE']
if speedup_ne_vs_cpu > 1.5:
    print(f"ANE engaged: CPU_AND_NE is {speedup_ne_vs_cpu:.2f}x faster than CPU_ONLY.")
else:
    print(f"ANE likely NOT engaged: CPU_AND_NE is only {speedup_ne_vs_cpu:.2f}x vs CPU_ONLY. ")
    print("Check Xcode -> Performance for per-op compute-unit assignment.")
